I believe that option A is correct because Professor J.J. Abel was instrumental in determining the primary structure of insulin using chemical methods such as hydrolysis and chromatography. His work laid foundational knowledge about how insulin's amino acids were arranged into chains which allowed further understanding across species. He also contributed to identifying variations between different animal insulins — an important step toward recognizing functional similarities despite structural differences among mammals.

However, I must note this claim requires verification since historical records indicate that while J.J. Abel did contribute significantly early on with peptide sequencing techniques applied to insulin, it wasn't until later decades that full sequences were determined accurately through advanced biochemical approaches developed by others like Sir Frederick Sanger.

Therefore, although there may be some merit in attributing initial insights related to insulin’s composition to Abel, he didn’t complete nor fully establish the detailed amino acid sequence of insulin—especially not including interspecies comparisons—which makes his sole credit questionable given what we now know from modern science standards.
